content:
Hi folks! I was wondering if you guys could help me with a problem I'm having. I have a code like the one below:

struct Object {
    a: Box<A>,
    b: B,
}

fn function(ptr: *mut A) {
   let obj = container_of!(&ptr, Self, timer) as *mut Object;
}

This code doesn't return the proper object from A. I wondered how I could get the obj from A, if A is boxed in the struct.

I don't think you can, container_of needs to be able to compute the offset from where the ptr is to the start of the struct. If you have a pointer to somewhere _else_, then there's no relationship between them. &ptr there is returning the address of somewhere on teh stack, so it's not correct.
Is this our own macro or is it from the compiler? It would be nice if it threw a compile error.

container_of has the following safety requirement: 

/// Callers must ensure that the pointer to the field is in fact a pointer to the specified field,
/// as opposed to a pointer to another object of the same type. If this condition is not met,
/// any dereference of the resulting pointer is UB.

As Alex pointed out, a pointer to A has no relationship to Object. Using container_of this way violates the requirement above; this isn't specific to Rust, it wouldn't work in C either.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Is this our own macro or is it from the compiler? It would be nice if it threw a compile error.

If we could detect such errors at compile time, this wouldn't have to be unsafe :)

The macro is less safe than it should be though, I don't think it's even trying to assert the type of the field? But even if it did, it might run into the auto-Deref soundness issue I ran into with place!() (where a Box<T> looks like a T).

This is the best I can come up with:

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! container_of {
    ($ptr:expr, $type:ty, $($f:tt)*) => {{
        let ptr = $ptr as *const u8;
        let offset = $crate::offset_of!($type, $($f)*);
        let outer = ptr.wrapping_offset(-offset) as *const $type;
        let inner = unsafe { core::ptr::addr_of!((*outer).$($f)*) };
        build_assert!(inner == $ptr);
        outer
    }}
}

It does the typecheck via the == comparison, but it still has the Box<T> hole:

pub struct Bar(u16);
pub struct Baz(Box<Bar>);

pub fn foo(a: *const u16) -> *const Baz {
    container_of!(a, Baz, 0.0)
}

Since doing the Deref through Box on an uninit struct is immediately UB even with it inside addr_of!(), it's not reliable. I got it to panic on the Rust playground in Release and segfault in Debug, but on arm64 in the kernel it just seems to return nothing (I guess the compiler notices what I'm doing is UB and decides it can do anything...), and no build error.
The memoffset crate uses let $type { $field: _, .. }; to check that the field exists.

bjorn3 said:

The memoffset crate uses let $type { $field: _, .. }; to check that the field exists.

But then that doesn't work for nested fields without calling the macro several times, right?
Indeed
how often do you need it with multiple fields?

also can we make this macro unsafe by adding

        unsafe fn container_of_is_unsafe() {}
        container_of_is_unsafe();

since the caller still needs to guarantee that the supplied pointer is actually pointing to the field of the given type.
I have a solution in mind but that requires a few unstable features (and not quite sound).

y86 said:

how often do you need it with multiple fields?

I have a use case for it in DRM GEM shmem (which I actually noticed after switching to the typechecked version above, since I was using container_of!() wrong but it was working by accident because the field was first in the object layout).

y86 said:

also can we make this macro unsafe by adding

        unsafe fn container_of_is_unsafe() {}
        container_of_is_unsafe();

since the caller still needs to guarantee that the supplied pointer is actually pointing to the field of the given type.

Yeah, I was thinking the same. The macro should be unsafe. Not because of the pointer requirement (since operating on pointers is safe, it's the deref after container_of!() that would be unsafe), but because of the Box<T> hole and the general ability for the macro to invoke arbitrary code on arbitrary pointers via auto-Deref.
Does your use case not work with multiple container_of calls?

Asahi Lina said:

Not because of the pointer requirement (since operating on pointers is safe, it's the deref after container_of!() that would be unsafe), but because of the Box<T> hole and the general ability for the macro to invoke arbitrary code on arbitrary pointers via auto-Deref.

I do not agree, the pointer requirement is definitely needed at the moment, since addr_of!((*ptr).field) is only allowed if ptr is a valid pointer.
We should fix the Box<T>/Deref hole before we put this upstream.

Asahi Lina said:

y86 said:

how often do you need it with multiple fields?

I have a use case for it in DRM GEM shmem (which I actually noticed after switching to the typechecked version above, since I was using container_of!() wrong but it was working by accident because the field was first in the object layout).

I have looked at the uses in this branch: https://github.com/AsahiLinux/linux/tree/gpu/rust-drm but I was not able to find any usages that have multiple fields. Is this the wrong branch?

Gary Guo said:

Does your use case not work with multiple container_of calls?

It does, it's just longer...

y86 said:

Asahi Lina said:

y86 said:

how often do you need it with multiple fields?

I have a use case for it in DRM GEM shmem (which I actually noticed after switching to the typechecked version above, since I was using container_of!() wrong but it was working by accident because the field was first in the object layout).

I have looked at the uses in this branch: https://github.com/AsahiLinux/linux/tree/gpu/rust-drm but I was not able to find any usages that have multiple fields. Is this the wrong branch?

It should have been multiple fields but it wasn't and the lack of type safety hid the mistake.

diff --git a/rust/kernel/drm/gem/shmem.rs b/rust/kernel/drm/gem/shmem.rs
index 8f17eba0be99..0d039ad1d0dd 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/drm/gem/shmem.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/drm/gem/shmem.rs
@@ -115,7 +115,7 @@ unsafe extern "C" fn gem_create_object<T: DriverObject>(

 unsafe extern "C" fn free_callback<T: DriverObject>(obj: *mut bindings::drm_gem_object) {
     // SAFETY: All of our objects are Object<T>.
-    let p = crate::container_of!(obj, Object<T>, obj) as *mut Object<T>;
+    let p = crate::container_of!(obj, Object<T>, obj.base) as *mut Object<T>;

     // SAFETY: p is never used after this
     unsafe {
@@ -239,7 +239,7 @@ impl<T: DriverObject> gem::IntoGEMObject for Object<T> {
     }

     fn from_gem_obj(obj: *mut bindings::drm_gem_object) -> *mut Object<T> {
-        crate::container_of!(obj, Object<T>, obj) as *mut Object<T>
+        crate::container_of!(obj, Object<T>, obj.base) as *mut Object<T>
     }
 }
 ```

If you syntactically limit it to a single field, I think my version is already free of the Box issue: the type check catches the Box case since auto-deref does not get invoked there. I was thinking of playing recursive macro games to support nested fields safely, but I only use that in two places so it's not a big deal if I have to call container_of!() twice either...

Asahi Lina said:

It should have been multiple fields but it wasn't and the lack of type safety hid the mistake.

Oh that makes sense.

Asahi Lina said:

If you syntactically limit it to a single field, I think my version is already free of the Box issue: the type check catches the Box case since auto-deref does not get invoked there. I was thinking of playing recursive macro games to support nested fields safely, but I only use that in two places so it's not a big deal if I have to call container_of!() twice either...

I think we should just fix offset_of! like bjorn suggested (and only allow a single field), since that might be also used elsewhere.
We could support multiple fields in offset_of! if we also require all field types to be given, i.e.

struct Foo {
    bar: Bar,
}
struct Bar {
    baz: Baz
}
struct Baz {
    data: usize,
}
offset_of!(Foo => Bar => Baz => usize, bar.baz.data);

Then we could just use let _: $type { $field: _, ..} = ...; to typecheck.

Another thing you could do is to create your own macro for this case only that calls container_of! twice.
I think for now it makes sense to just limit both macros to a single field, and then I think with my type check assert we should be good?

I think we only need to adjust offset_of!, because if that is correct container_of! should also be correct. Not sure if your fix works with that though? If not, we can just use the let _: $type { $f: _, .. } trick.
